summary:
The described condition indicates an increase in interdental sulcus depth leading to a gingival pocket; this is not associated directly with alveolar crest level alterations but rather involves only soft-tissue displacement from its normal position relative to the cementoenamel junction. Thus, it corresponds most closely to option \(\textbf{(B)}\) as indicated by my previous response based purely upon provided context about epithelial attachments moving towards root surfaces while leaving behind exposed areas that form new potential sites for plaque accumulation if left untreated over time which can lead further complications such as calculus deposition etc... To reiterate succinctly:

Apical migration causes increased sulcular depths forming gingival pockets; thus,\( \boxed{\text{Gingival Pocket Formation}}.\) Summary: Apical migration of the epithelial attachment accompanied by marginal gingival recession creates deepened spaces known as gingival pockets, indicating altered positioning of soft tissues away from their original positions near the enamel-cementum junction. \( \boxed{\text{Gingival pocket formation}} \).
